paragraphs.body: When You Realized that Squirrel Hill Does Not Have Squirrel Sometimes the name of a place does not represent the true view of that place. So there are a lot of fun to discover different places that have interesting names and to compare the real face of them with our inmagination. Besides this, the places with identical purpose in differen areas or countries may have opposite views because of diverse cultural backgrounds. In this essay, the main idea I want to express is that we should celebrate differences and try to understand and accept it.

Before I visited Squirrel Hill, I thought it was a small moutain covered by thick pine trees and lived a lot of squirrels. Apparently I get this idea from the name “Squirel Hill”, which is very straightforward. I also thought this place may be a natural reserve and a good place for camping and strolling.

When I really visited Squirrel Hill, I found that this is way more different than a natural reserve, it is a community. But it is indeed a hill with no doubt. What surprised me most is that I cannot find any single squirrel in Squirrel Hill. Maybe I am unlucky but it happened to me. The only wield animal I saw in this place is a deer.

What made Squirrel Hill and my hometown different was the structure of the residence area. Residence in Pittsburgh, or American, is more seperate and less dense than China. They always consist of some short apartment and houses which cannot live as much people as China. There are also many small shops surrounded the residence, so it is convenient to live in the community like this. Common residence in China is apartment complex which consists of numerous apartment buildings and typically with a small park and a playground.

The huge difference between American comunity and Chinese comunity is because of the enormous population density disparity. The situation that China has large population size can account for this difference.

Cross cultural Comparison Observing the differences in residential structures helped me appreciate how cultural and societal factors influence urban planning. For example, the naming of “Squirrel Hill” shows how names can sometimes carry symbolic or historical significance rather than being literal.
